questions.question: What is the strongest first move in a consumer-backend mock?
options: Define the main user-visible promise and the dominant workload shape before drawing components.
Start with the cache layer because most consumer products need one.
Assume the answer is a feed system unless told otherwise.
Spend most of the time listing storage options.
questions.explanation: A clear product promise and workload shape keep the architecture from becoming generic.

questions.question: Why does producer or room skew matter so much in consumer systems?
options: Because amplification from a few hot users or hot rooms can dominate fanout, storage, and queue behavior.
Because skew matters only in analytics systems.
Because skew removes the need to estimate QPS.
Because high skew always means the system should use strong consistency everywhere.
questions.explanation: Hot producers and hot groups often create the real bottleneck in consumer backends.

questions.question: What is the main benefit of naming one unacceptable user-visible failure early?
options: It helps prioritize which path needs stronger guarantees and which anomalies are tolerable.
It guarantees there will be no follow-up questions.
It replaces the need for architecture diagrams.
It means every subsystem must use the same latency target.
questions.explanation: The least acceptable failure usually reveals which parts of the system deserve the most budget.

questions.question: Which signal best detects that a fanout design is failing under skew?
options: Backlog growth and per-producer amplification metrics on the async distribution path.
Only average CPU across the whole fleet.
The number of teams using the service.
Total count of database tables.
questions.explanation: Skew problems show up most clearly in segmented amplification and backlog metrics.

questions.question: Why should a strong answer choose one source-of-truth boundary explicitly?
options: Because duplicate, stale, and missing-state trade-offs depend on where correctness is enforced.
Because interviews always require exactly one database.
Because source-of-truth decisions matter only for financial products.
Because caches become irrelevant once the boundary is named.
questions.explanation: Without a clear state boundary, consistency claims remain hand-wavy.

questions.question: What is the main trade-off of pushing more work onto asynchronous pipelines?
options: The system can scale better and protect tail latency, but freshness and operational debugging often get harder.
It guarantees strict ordering for all users.
It removes the need for degraded modes.
It makes hot producers irrelevant.
questions.explanation: Async work placement helps throughput, but it usually introduces lag and more operational surfaces.

questions.question: If the interviewer changes the top priority from freshness to cost, what is the strongest response?
options: Revisit caching, precomputation, and fanout strategy while naming which user-visible behavior becomes weaker.
Keep the same design and only change storage vendor names.
Remove the observability section to save time.
Assume cost and freshness are never related.
questions.explanation: A changed priority should trigger concrete architecture and guarantee changes.

questions.question: Why is an explicit degraded mode important in a consumer mock answer?
options: It shows the product can fail in a controlled way instead of pretending every path is equally critical.
It proves the system never loses availability.
It means the design no longer needs retries.
It is only helpful for edge networks, not backends.
questions.explanation: Senior answers describe what the user sees when the system is stressed, not only when it is healthy.
